documents.content: Realistic interview preparation timelines based on what's worked for engineers I've coached: For new grad / SDE-1 with no prior interview prep: 3-4 months of consistent daily practice (1-2 hours weekdays, 4-5 hours weekends). Focus 70% on LeetCode patterns, 20% on basic system design, 10% on behavioral story prep. For SDE-2 with 2-3 years experience: 2-3 months. The system design weight increases to 35-40%, behavioral to 15-20%. For senior+ roles: 1-2 months if you're already strong on coding; the bulk of prep is system design at scale and leadership stories. Burning out from a long prep cycle is more common than under-preparing — pace yourself, take rest days, and don't grind LeetCode if you've already done 200 problems.

Week-by-week prep plan for a backend SDE-2 with 3 months until first interview: Weeks 1-2 — review fundamentals (arrays, strings, hash maps, two pointers). 5 easy + 3 medium LeetCode per day. Weeks 3-4 — trees, graphs, BFS/DFS. Mix of easy and medium problems. Weeks 5-6 — dynamic programming. This is where most people struggle; spend extra time on the classic patterns (knapsack, LCS, LIS). Weeks 7-8 — system design fundamentals. Read DDIA chapters 1-9. Watch ByteByteGo videos. Practice 5-6 designs end to end. Weeks 9-10 — mock interviews on Pramp or with peers. This is non-negotiable; you cannot self-assess your communication. Weeks 11-12 — behavioral story prep (build 10 STAR stories), company research, applying. Don't apply to your top choice first; warm up with companies you're less attached to.

Resources that gave me the highest ROI per hour spent: NeetCode 150 (free YouTube + paid practice site) — the curated problem list is more efficient than grinding random LeetCode. Educative.io's 'Grokking the Coding Interview' for pattern-based learning. ByteByteGo for system design at multiple levels. 'Designing Data-Intensive Applications' (DDIA) book for depth. Pramp and interviewing.io for mock interviews with real practitioners. For behavioral prep, read 'Cracking the Coding Interview' chapter on behavioral and use your own past projects as raw material. Avoid: random YouTube channels with generic advice, paying for expensive bootcamps when free resources are better, doing 500 LeetCode problems without understanding the patterns.
